name: GPT-5.4 nano
archDisplay: Undisclosed architecture (presumed dense transformer); cheapest/fastest tier of the GPT-5.4 family, targeted at classification, extraction, ranking, and lightweight coding subagents; lacks native computer-use tool support that mini/flagship have
note: Cheapest tier of the GPT-5.4 family, launched Mar 17 2026 alongside GPT-5.4 mini via OpenAI's own announcement as a distinct, separately-priced ($0.20/$1.25 vs GPT-5 nano's $0.05 per-M input per OpenAI's own pricing-comparison table), separately-benchmarked model -- not a repricing of GPT-5 nano. API-only (no ChatGPT/Codex availability); OpenAI's own model card lists computer-use as 'Not supported' for this tier (unlike mini/flagship).

results.notes: SWE-Bench Pro (Public), xhigh reasoning effort. Cross-confirmed verbatim by datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano.
Terminal-Bench 2.0, xhigh reasoning effort. Cross-confirmed verbatim by datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano.
GPQA Diamond, xhigh reasoning effort. datacamp.com independently notes GPT-5.4 nano scores 9.8 points higher than Claude Haiku 4.5 on this benchmark, consistent with this figure.
OSWorld-Verified, xhigh reasoning effort -- weaker than GPT-5 mini's 42.0% here since nano isn't tuned for computer-use/vision tasks. Cross-confirmed verbatim by datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano.
Humanity's Last Exam, no tools, xhigh reasoning effort (with-tools variant scores 37.7%).
tau2-bench (telecom), xhigh reasoning effort. datacamp.com independently notes GPT-5.4 nano scores 9.5 points higher than Claude Haiku 4.5 on this benchmark, consistent with this figure.
MMMU-Pro (standard, no Python tool; the 'w/ Python' variant scores 69.5%), xhigh reasoning effort.

GDPval-AA v2, independently measured by Artificial Analysis on the 'GPT-5.4 nano (xhigh)' model page (AA's page confirms xhigh effort, matching this catalog entry's existing xhigh-attributed self-reported results). Not previously tracked here. [score corrected during round-6 completeness verify: proposed 30 -> 1100] [score corrected during round-12 accuracy re-verify: 1100 -> 30, per verifier: Loaded artificialanalysis.ai/models/gpt-5-4-nano live in browser (WebFetch's markdown conversion drops the chart values, so a raw page-text read was required to get real numbers). The GDPval-AA v2 ranked bar chart lists 29 models 0-100%; counting pos] [SCALE FIX 2026-07-17: corrected from a normalized percentage (30%) back to this taxonomy's actual raw 0-2000-point Elo scale. This model's own note already documents an earlier round-6 finding of raw=1100 that a later round-12 adversarial re-verify wrongly 'corrected' back down to a percentage, not realizing this benchmark's dominant corpus convention is raw Elo, not a 0-100 normalized score. Restoring the original round-6 raw reading.]

IFBench (instruction following), independently measured by Artificial Analysis on the 'GPT-5.4 nano (xhigh)' model page. Not previously tracked here. (AA's independently re-measured Terminal-Bench v2.1 (61%), GPQA Diamond (82%), HLE (26%), and MMMU-Pro (65%) for this model were checked but all duplicate benchmark slugs already tracked on this entry from OpenAI's own launch post -- skipped as non-novel per instructions, despite some numeric divergence from the self-reported figures.)

verificationNotes:
Existence and distinctness from GPT-5 nano confirmed by directly fetching OpenAI's own official launch post (openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-4-mini-and-nano/, dated Mar 17 2026), which states GPT-5.4 nano 'is also a significant upgrade over GPT-5 nano' and gives it its own pricing tier below GPT-5.4 mini in OpenAI's own quick-comparison table (GPT-5.4 mini $0.75 vs GPT-5.4 nano $0.20 vs GPT-5 nano $0.05, all per-M-input). Pricing ($0.20/$1.25 per M), 400K context, and 128K max output independently confirmed via OpenAI's live developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4-nano model card (own snapshot dated gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17, matching the announcement date), which also documents that Computer Use and Tool Search are 'Not supported' for this tier specifically (unlike mini/flagship). Independent secondary source datacamp.com/blog/gpt-5-4-mini-nano (fetched directly) corroborates the same benchmark table verbatim and separately compares nano to Claude Haiku 4.5 on GPQA Diamond and tau2-bench-telecom, both consistent with the scores here. All 7 result rows taken from OpenAI's own multi-table benchmark breakdown; Toolathlon and MCP Atlas scores were also published but omitted since no corpus benchmark slug matches them.

[2026-07-16 gap-fill pass] Researched the 7 flagged core-benchmark gaps (mmlu, math, aime, humaneval, livecodebench, swe-bench, mmmu) via three source types: (1) OpenAI's own GPT-5.4 family launch materials -- re-checked not just this model's launch post but all three sibling entries in this family (gpt-5-4.json, gpt-5-4-mini.json, gpt-5-4-pro.json), none of which report mmlu, math, humaneval, livecodebench, base-swe-bench, or base-mmmu for ANY tier, confirming this is a family-wide reporting choice (OpenAI's GPT-5.4-era benchmark tables favor GPQA/HLE/SWE-Bench-Pro/Terminal-Bench/tau2-bench/MMMU-Pro/ARC-AGI/FrontierMath/BrowseComp/GDPval over the older suite), not a nano-specific gap. (2) Artificial Analysis's live model page (artificialanalysis.ai/models/gpt-5-4-nano) -- read the full rendered DOM text (all Intelligence-evaluation entries: GDPval-AA v2, tau3-Banking, Terminal-Bench v2.1, SciCode, HLE, GPQA Diamond, CritPt, AA-Omniscience, AA-LCR, AA-Briefcase, Harvey LAB-AA, EnterpriseOps-Gym-AA, IFBench, APEX-Agents-AA, ITBench-AA, MMMU-Pro) and confirmed via in-page JS string search that "MMLU", "MATH-500", "HumanEval", "LiveCodeBench", "SWE-bench"/"SWE-Bench" do not appear anywhere on the page. (3) Epoch AI's own model page (epoch.ai/models/gpt-5-4-nano) -- Epoch runs its own independent evals across Reasoning/Agents/Coding/Math&Science/Multimodal/Misc categories; the full evaluated-benchmark list for this model (WeirdML, APEX-Agents, SciCode, ALE-Bench, OTIS Mock AIME, GPQA Diamond, FrontierMath T1-3/T4, CritPt, ProofBench, ARC-AGI-1/2, Chess Puzzles, SimpleQA Verified, ForecastBench) contains no MMLU, MATH, HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, SWE-bench, or base MMMU entry; checking a sibling GPT-5.x model's page on the same site confirmed Epoch explicitly catalogs MMLU as a tracked-but-"Not evaluated" benchmark for this model generation rather than silently dropping it, supporting that these are genuine absences (consistent with frontier labs no longer running/publishing largely-saturated legacy benchmarks) rather than a research gap on my part. mmlu, math, humaneval, livecodebench, swe-bench, and mmmu are therefore recorded as omitted_no_evidence. The aime gap resolved positively: Epoch AI's page gives OTIS Mock AIME 2024-2025 = 87.8% (90% CI 83.9-91.7%) for GPT-5.4 nano, cross-confirmed verbatim by themodelbeat.com's relay of the same data -- added as the 'aime' result.

[2026-07-18 gap-fill pass: livecodebench, swe-bench, mmmu] Chased all three flagged current-generation gaps.

livecodebench and base swe-bench (SWE-bench Verified) RESOLVED POSITIVELY via Vals.ai's dedicated GPT-5.4 nano model page (vals.ai/models/openai_gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17) -- confirmed correct model identity by matching release date (3/17/2026), 400K context window, and 128K max output, all exact matches to this file's spec fields. Vals.ai renders benchmark scores client-side (WebFetch's markdown conversion AND a live browser page-text read both show a static '0.0%' placeholder for every row), so the true values were extracted via in-browser JS reading each benchmark row's CSS --target-width custom property: LiveCodeBench = 84.009% (rank 30/127) and SWE-bench = 69.8% (rank 49/71). This is the identical extraction technique already used and validated for the GPT-5.4 flagship (livecodebench 84.1%, swe-bench 76.9%) and GPT-5.4 mini (livecodebench 81.47%, swe-bench 73.0%) sibling entries elsewhere in this corpus. Before use, directly navigated to vals.ai's own dedicated benchmark pages to rule out mislabeling: /benchmarks/lcb is genuinely titled 'LiveCodeBench' and /benchmarks/swebench is genuinely titled 'SWE-bench Verified' -- neither is a relabeled/different benchmark masquerading under a similar name.

Caught and rejected a false lead during this pass: a WebSearch result synthesis asserted "BenchLM lists SWE-bench Verified = 69.3 for GPT-5.4 nano." Direct verification (a full accessibility-tree/DOM read of benchlm.ai/models/gpt-5-4-nano via browser, not just WebFetch's lossy markdown conversion) shows this model's BenchLM Coding category contains exactly 3 benchmarks -- Vibe Code Bench v1.1 (26.10%), AA Coding Index (56.1%), AA-SciCode (46.9%) -- and no LiveCodeBench or SWE-bench row exists on the page at all. The '69.3' figure was a WebSearch-summary hallucination, not a real citable BenchLM data point (notably close to, but distinct from, the real 69.8% independently confirmed via Vals.ai). Two further WebSearch-only claims this pass ("MMMU: 42/76" and "LiveCodeBench: 27/122" attributed to aiproductivity.ai and lmcouncil.ai respectively) were also checked and rejected after directly fetching both pages and confirming neither contains any GPT-5.4 nano row at all. Lesson applied throughout this pass: WebSearch synthesis for this model is unreliable and every numeric claim was checked against a direct fetch/render of the actually-cited page before use.

mmmu (base, non-Pro) OMITTED as omitted_no_evidence. This model's modalities include vision and capabilities.vision=true, so MMMU is not architecturally inapplicable here -- but no genuine base-MMMU score could be found after checking: (1) OpenAI's own launch post and model card, already established elsewhere in this file's notes to report only MMMU-Pro / MMMU-Pro w/ Python for this and every other GPT-5.4-family tier, never base MMMU; (2) BenchLM's full Multimodal-category evidence for this model (exactly 3 benchmarks -- MMMU-Pro 66.1%, MMMU-Pro w/ Python 69.5%, AA-MMMU-Pro 65.4% -- confirmed via direct DOM read, all MMMU-Pro variants, no base-MMMU row); (3) Artificial Analysis's model page (same -- only AA-MMMU-Pro is tracked, already excluded from this file as a duplicate slug); (4) Vals.ai's nano model page, which does show a row labeled 'MMMU' (73.584%, rank 47/82) -- but directly navigating to Vals.ai's own /benchmarks/mmmu benchmark page shows its page title is actually 'MMMU Pro', confirming (consistent with an identical finding already documented in this corpus's GPT-5.4 mini sibling entry) that Vals.ai's 'MMMU' column is really MMMU-Pro under a shortened UI label, not the base benchmark -- so this figure was correctly excluded from the 'mmmu' slug rather than misfiled; (5) targeted arXiv/Hugging Face searches for a GPT-5.4-nano MMMU baseline returned no model-specific hits, only unrelated papers. Five source types checked (exceeding the required 4-of-6 minimum), consistent absence throughout -- base MMMU appears to be a benchmark this entire model generation has not published (MMMU-Pro is the family's chosen multimodal metric instead), not a research gap specific to nano.

Side note, out of scope for this file so no value changed here: this pass's direct confirmation that Vals.ai's 'MMMU' UI label actually points to MMMU-Pro raises a possible data-quality concern in the sibling gpt-5-4.json (GPT-5.4 flagship) entry, which carries a base 'mmmu'=87.5 score sourced to Vals.ai and described in its own notes as a genuine base-MMMU extraction -- that may itself be a mislabeled MMMU-Pro figure, given the mini sibling explicitly caught and avoided this exact trap. Flagged separately for a follow-up pass on that file; not corrected here.
